content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


July 18, 2016


Albert Yang
City Attorney's Office
250 Hamilton Avenue, 8th Floor
Palo Alto, CA 94301

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-140

Dear Mr. Yang:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Commissioners Eric Rosenblum and Kate Downing of the City of Palo Alto's Planning and Transportation Commission regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Moreover, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTIONS

1. Is Commissioner Rosenblum prohibited from taking part in a decision of whether to recommend to the city council changes to the City's zoning that would disfavor commercial development where his employer, Palantir Technologies, Inc. ("Palantir"), leases and occupies multiple commercial buildings in the area?

2. Is Commissioner Downing prohibited from taking part in the decision where her spouse is employed by Palantir?

CONCLUSIONS

1 and 2. No. The decision will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on their interests.

___________________________

1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-140
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are the City Attorney for the City of Palo Alto and are requesting advice on behalf of
Commissioners Eric Rosenblum and Kate Downing of the City of Palo Alto's Planning and
Transportation Commission ("Commission").

The Palo Alto City Council is considering zoning changes that would disfavor commercial
development in the University Avenue downtown area. The Commission will be making
recommendations to the city council regarding these changes. These changes would have an impact
on the availability and rental rates of commercial office space in downtown Palo Alto.

Commissioner Rosenblum is employed by Palantir. Commissioner Downing's spouse is
employed by Palantir. Palantir is a privately held multinational technology company valued at $20
billion that is headquartered in downtown Palo Alto where it occupies multiple office buildings.

The company has offices throughout North America, Europe, the Middle East, Asia,
Australia and New Zealand. "The company controls about 250,000 square feet of office space, or
10 to 15 percent of the commercial inventory [in downtown Palo Alto], spread over 23 or so
buildings. Some of its leases are for a decade or longer. Annual revenue topped $1.5 billion in 2015,
sources say, meaning Palantir is bigger than top publicly traded cloud software companies like
Workday and Service Now. It has 1,800 employees and is growing headcount 30 percent annually
said the sources…"2 Palantir's business is unrelated to real estate. You would like to know whether
Commissioners Rosenblum and Downing may participate in the decisions related to the zoning
changes near Palantir's headquarters in downtown Palo Alto.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
(Section 87103.) Section 87103 defines interests from which a conflict of interest may arise. They
include the following:

• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status,
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

• Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

Palantir is an interest of Commissioner Rosenblum as his employer and source of income.
Commissioner Downing has a financial interest in Palantir as a source of income due to her
spouse's employment with the company. ("Income" includes any community property interest in
the income of a spouse (Section 82030).)

_______________________

2 http://cnbc.com/2016/01/12/the-cia-backed-start-up-thats-takin-over-palo-alto.html.

File No. A-16-140
Page No. 3

Foreseeability and Materiality: The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. Regulation 18701 provides: "A financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's agency." None of the Commissioners' financial interests are explicitly involved in the decision to recommend the zoning changes because Palantir is not the subject of or a named party in the governmental decisions. Thus, pursuant to Regulation 18701(h), if a financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable, however, if the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable.

Materiality will be found under Regulation 18702.3(a)(4) if:

• The source is a business entity and a prudent person with sufficient information would find it reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the value of a privately-held business entity. (Regulation 18702.1(b).)

You state that Palantir is a "multi-billion company" and has offices throughout North America, Europe, the Middle East, Asia, Australia and New Zealand. You cite a Forbes article stating that Palantir was recently valued at $20 billion. According to a source quoted in the CNBC article, the company has 1,800 employees and its 2015 revenues topped $1.5 billion. The sheer size of Palantir's business as well as the breadth of its worldwide presence suggest that a prudent person would not find it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of a decision affecting the value of commercial property leases in downtown Palo Alto would contribute to a change in the value of Palantir's business. Under these facts, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the zoning decisions you have described will have a material financial effect on Commissioners Rosenblum's and Downing's interests.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

[Signature]

By:   Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

VJ:jgl
